title: Parkinson's Disease
oneLiner: Progressive neurodegenerative disorder from striatal dopamine deficiency; presents with bradykinesia, tremor, rigidity; common in elderly; leads to falls, aspiration, immobility.

sections.Parkinson’s Disease: **One-liner:** Progressive neurodegenerative disorder from striatal dopamine deficiency; presents with bradykinesia, tremor, rigidity; common in elderly; leads to falls, aspiration, immobility.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS)** – triggered by abrupt withdrawal of dopaminergic drugs OR introduction of dopamine antagonists (e.g. antipsychotics, metoclopramide).  
  *Single finding:* **rigidity + hyperthermia (>38°C) + altered consciousness** – check recent medication changes immediately.
- **Aspiration Pneumonia** – due to dysphagia and impaired cough reflex.  
  *Single finding:* **new oxygen requirement + audible wet voice/sputum** – do bedside swallow screen before giving oral meds or food.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Bradykinesia (slowness, reduced arm swing, festinant gait)
  - Resting tremor (pill-rolling, asymmetrical, reduces with voluntary movement)
  - Rigidity (cogwheel or lead-pipe on passive movement)
  - Postural instability (usually late – check pull test)
  - Non-motor: anosmia, REM sleep behaviour disorder, constipation, depression
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Masked facies, hypophonia, micrographia
  - Shuffling gait, short steps, difficulty turning
  - Positive glabellar tap (Myerson’s sign)
  - Assess for orthostatic hypotension (especially if on antihypertensives or PD drugs)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Essential tremor** – bilateral postural/action tremor, no bradykinesia or rigidity, improves with alcohol
- **Drug-induced parkinsonism** – symmetrical, history of antipsychotics / metoclopramide / lithium, resolves on stopping drug
- **Vascular parkinsonism** – lower body predominant, no rest tremor, stepwise decline, vascular risk factors
- **Multiple system atrophy (MSA)** – poor levodopa response, early autonomic failure (postural BP drop, urinary incontinence), cerebellar signs
- **Progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP)** – vertical gaze palsy, early falls, symmetric axial rigidity
- **Splitter:** **Dramatic levodopa response** – good and sustained improvement favours idiopathic PD over atypical parkinsonism
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Swallow screen (water swallow test – any cough/choking = high aspiration risk)
  - Timed up-and-go test (assess fall risk)
  - Orthostatic BP (lying + 1 min + 3 min standing – drop >20 mmHg systolic)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, CK (if NMS suspected), TFT (to rule out thyroid causes of tremor)
  - Vitamin B12 (deficiency mimics parkinsonism)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: no routine imaging needed if classic presentation
  - Second-line: MRI brain (if atypical features – to exclude stroke, normal pressure hydrocephalus, vascular lesions)
- **Special tests:** DaTscan (SPECT) – reserved for diagnostic uncertainty; shows reduced striatal dopamine transporter binding in PD
- **Diagnostic criteria:** UK Parkinson’s Disease Society Brain Bank Criteria – bradykinesia + ≥1 of rest tremor / rigidity / postural instability + excluding other causes + clear levodopa response
- **Never-miss test:** **Serum CK** in any patient with altered consciousness, rigidity, and fever – to rule out NMS. Also **pregnancy test** in women of childbearing age before certain drugs (e.g. anticholinergics).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If suspected NMS: stop all dopamine antagonists, start IV fluids, cooling, consider dantrolene or bromocriptine [check local policy]; call ICU.
  - If aspiration: NBM, IV antibiotics (e.g. co-amoxiclav), oxygen, speech therapy review.
  - Ensure continuation of PD medications – do not stop at home unless NMS suspected.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Medication reconciliation – ensure PD drugs given on time (delays = deterioration)
  - Physiotherapy (mobility, falls prevention); speech therapy (swallow assessment, communication aids)
  - Bowel regimen – lactulose or senna for constipation
  - Avoid antipsychotics (except quetiapine or clozapine in severe psychosis) – haloperidol/olanzapine can worsen parkinsonism
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Refer to PD nurse specialist and neurologist
  - Consider advanced therapies: deep brain stimulation (DBS), apomorphine infusion, duodopa (levodopa/carbidopa intestinal gel)
  - Manage non-motor symptoms: orthostatic hypotension (midodrine, fludrocortisone), depression (SSRIs), sleep disorders
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline if nil by mouth (e.g. aspiration risk) – rate based on hydration status and renal function
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin (if immobile and no contraindication)
  - Diet: soft / pureed if dysphagia; NBM if aspiration risk until swallow review
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly; more often if unstable or on IV fluids
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Levodopa/carbidopa (co-careldopa – Sinemet) or levodopa/benserazide (co-beneldopa – Madopar)
  - *Starting dose:* Sinemet 62.5 mg (50 mg levodopa + 12.5 mg carbidopa) three times daily [check local policy]
  - *Titration:* slowly increase over weeks – target symptom control without dyskinesias
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Dopamine agonists: ropinirole (start 0.25 mg TDS), pramipexole (start 0.088 mg TDS)
  - MAO-B inhibitors: selegiline (5 mg BD), rasagiline (1 mg OD)
  - COMT inhibitors: entacapone (200 mg with each levodopa dose)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Dopamine agonists: avoid in impulse control disorders or psychosis
  - MAO-B inhibitors: avoid with SSRIs/SNRIs (serotonin syndrome risk)
  - Antipsychotics: all (except quetiapine/clozapine) block dopamine – will worsen motor symptoms
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Levodopa – no significant adjustment needed; dopamine agonists – adjust in renal impairment (e.g. pramipexole halve dose in CrCl 30-50)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Abruptly stopping levodopa or dopamine agonist** – can precipitate NMS. Always ensure hospital pharmacy supplies the patient’s usual brand and dose.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (see above)
  - Aspiration pneumonia
  - Falls and fractures (especially hip / wrist)
  - Acute confusion / delirium (often from infection or drug change)
- **Chronic:**
  - Motor fluctuations (wearing-off, on-off, dyskinesias)
  - Medication-induced dyskinesias (peak-dose or biphasic)
  - Dementia (LBV) – up to 80% in advanced stages
  - Postural hypotension, falls
  - Constipation, sialorrhoea, sleep disorders
sections.Prognosis:
- Progressive but variable; median survival ~10-15 years from diagnosis
- Good initial response to levodopa; motor complications develop after 5–10 years
- Poor prognostic features: older age at onset, early postural instability/falls, poor levodopa response, early dementia
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Give PD meds ON TIME** – every dose should be given at the exact time scheduled; even 15 minutes late can cause deterioration. Use a drug chart alarm.
- **Check for orthostatic hypotension** in all patients – common cause of falls; treat with increased salt/water, review antihypertensives, consider midodrine.
- **Refer to Parkinson’s nurse specialist** – they can coordinate care, adjust medications, and provide education.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not stop PD medications** (even NBM) without discussing with neurology – consider IV/transdermal alternatives (apomorphine, rotigotine patch).
- **Do not use typical antipsychotics** (haloperidol, chlorpromazine, droperidol) for delirium or psychosis – they will worsen parkinsonism and risk NMS. Use quetiapine or clozapine only after specialist input.
- **Do not dismiss new confusion/fall** as “just part of PD” – rule out infection (UTI, pneumonia), silent aspiration, or NMS.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Fever or rigors (possible aspiration or NMS)
  - Worsening confusion, drowsiness, or rigidity
  - Inability to swallow oral medications or keep food down
  - New falls or difficulty walking
- **Follow-up:** Parkinson’s clinic within 2–4 weeks; sooner if on newly started medication or after hospitalisation.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 or rising lactate
  - GCS drop ≤ 12 (suspect NMS, stroke, or sepsis)
  - Hyperthermia + rigidity + altered mental status (suspected NMS)
  - Cannot protect airway (aspiration, severe cough impairment)

rawMarkdown:
## Parkinson’s Disease
**One-liner:** Progressive neurodegenerative disorder from striatal dopamine deficiency; presents with bradykinesia, tremor, rigidity; common in elderly; leads to falls, aspiration, immobility.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS)** – triggered by abrupt withdrawal of dopaminergic drugs OR introduction of dopamine antagonists (e.g. antipsychotics, metoclopramide).  
  *Single finding:* **rigidity + hyperthermia (>38°C) + altered consciousness** – check recent medication changes immediately.
- **Aspiration Pneumonia** – due to dysphagia and impaired cough reflex.  
  *Single finding:* **new oxygen requirement + audible wet voice/sputum** – do bedside swallow screen before giving oral meds or food.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Bradykinesia (slowness, reduced arm swing, festinant gait)
  - Resting tremor (pill-rolling, asymmetrical, reduces with voluntary movement)
  - Rigidity (cogwheel or lead-pipe on passive movement)
  - Postural instability (usually late – check pull test)
  - Non-motor: anosmia, REM sleep behaviour disorder, constipation, depression
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Masked facies, hypophonia, micrographia
  - Shuffling gait, short steps, difficulty turning
  - Positive glabellar tap (Myerson’s sign)
  - Assess for orthostatic hypotension (especially if on antihypertensives or PD drugs)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Essential tremor** – bilateral postural/action tremor, no bradykinesia or rigidity, improves with alcohol
- **Drug-induced parkinsonism** – symmetrical, history of antipsychotics / metoclopramide / lithium, resolves on stopping drug
- **Vascular parkinsonism** – lower body predominant, no rest tremor, stepwise decline, vascular risk factors
- **Multiple system atrophy (MSA)** – poor levodopa response, early autonomic failure (postural BP drop, urinary incontinence), cerebellar signs
- **Progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP)** – vertical gaze palsy, early falls, symmetric axial rigidity
- **Splitter:** **Dramatic levodopa response** – good and sustained improvement favours idiopathic PD over atypical parkinsonism

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Swallow screen (water swallow test – any cough/choking = high aspiration risk)
  - Timed up-and-go test (assess fall risk)
  - Orthostatic BP (lying + 1 min + 3 min standing – drop >20 mmHg systolic)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, CK (if NMS suspected), TFT (to rule out thyroid causes of tremor)
  - Vitamin B12 (deficiency mimics parkinsonism)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: no routine imaging needed if classic presentation
  - Second-line: MRI brain (if atypical features – to exclude stroke, normal pressure hydrocephalus, vascular lesions)
- **Special tests:** DaTscan (SPECT) – reserved for diagnostic uncertainty; shows reduced striatal dopamine transporter binding in PD
- **Diagnostic criteria:** UK Parkinson’s Disease Society Brain Bank Criteria – bradykinesia + ≥1 of rest tremor / rigidity / postural instability + excluding other causes + clear levodopa response
- **Never-miss test:** **Serum CK** in any patient with altered consciousness, rigidity, and fever – to rule out NMS. Also **pregnancy test** in women of childbearing age before certain drugs (e.g. anticholinergics).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If suspected NMS: stop all dopamine antagonists, start IV fluids, cooling, consider dantrolene or bromocriptine [check local policy]; call ICU.
  - If aspiration: NBM, IV antibiotics (e.g. co-amoxiclav), oxygen, speech therapy review.
  - Ensure continuation of PD medications – do not stop at home unless NMS suspected.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Medication reconciliation – ensure PD drugs given on time (delays = deterioration)
  - Physiotherapy (mobility, falls prevention); speech therapy (swallow assessment, communication aids)
  - Bowel regimen – lactulose or senna for constipation
  - Avoid antipsychotics (except quetiapine or clozapine in severe psychosis) – haloperidol/olanzapine can worsen parkinsonism
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Refer to PD nurse specialist and neurologist
  - Consider advanced therapies: deep brain stimulation (DBS), apomorphine infusion, duodopa (levodopa/carbidopa intestinal gel)
  - Manage non-motor symptoms: orthostatic hypotension (midodrine, fludrocortisone), depression (SSRIs), sleep disorders
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline if nil by mouth (e.g. aspiration risk) – rate based on hydration status and renal function
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin (if immobile and no contraindication)
  - Diet: soft / pureed if dysphagia; NBM if aspiration risk until swallow review
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly; more often if unstable or on IV fluids

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Levodopa/carbidopa (co-careldopa – Sinemet) or levodopa/benserazide (co-beneldopa – Madopar)
  - *Starting dose:* Sinemet 62.5 mg (50 mg levodopa + 12.5 mg carbidopa) three times daily [check local policy]
  - *Titration:* slowly increase over weeks – target symptom control without dyskinesias
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Dopamine agonists: ropinirole (start 0.25 mg TDS), pramipexole (start 0.088 mg TDS)
  - MAO-B inhibitors: selegiline (5 mg BD), rasagiline (1 mg OD)
  - COMT inhibitors: entacapone (200 mg with each levodopa dose)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Dopamine agonists: avoid in impulse control disorders or psychosis
  - MAO-B inhibitors: avoid with SSRIs/SNRIs (serotonin syndrome risk)
  - Antipsychotics: all (except quetiapine/clozapine) block dopamine – will worsen motor symptoms
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Levodopa – no significant adjustment needed; dopamine agonists – adjust in renal impairment (e.g. pramipexole halve dose in CrCl 30-50)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Abruptly stopping levodopa or dopamine agonist** – can precipitate NMS. Always ensure hospital pharmacy supplies the patient’s usual brand and dose.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (see above)
  - Aspiration pneumonia
  - Falls and fractures (especially hip / wrist)
  - Acute confusion / delirium (often from infection or drug change)
- **Chronic:**
  - Motor fluctuations (wearing-off, on-off, dyskinesias)
  - Medication-induced dyskinesias (peak-dose or biphasic)
  - Dementia (LBV) – up to 80% in advanced stages
  - Postural hypotension, falls
  - Constipation, sialorrhoea, sleep disorders

### Prognosis
- Progressive but variable; median survival ~10-15 years from diagnosis
- Good initial response to levodopa; motor complications develop after 5–10 years
- Poor prognostic features: older age at onset, early postural instability/falls, poor levodopa response, early dementia

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Give PD meds ON TIME** – every dose should be given at the exact time scheduled; even 15 minutes late can cause deterioration. Use a drug chart alarm.
- **Check for orthostatic hypotension** in all patients – common cause of falls; treat with increased salt/water, review antihypertensives, consider midodrine.
- **Refer to Parkinson’s nurse specialist** – they can coordinate care, adjust medications, and provide education.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not stop PD medications** (even NBM) without discussing with neurology – consider IV/transdermal alternatives (apomorphine, rotigotine patch).
- **Do not use typical antipsychotics** (haloperidol, chlorpromazine, droperidol) for delirium or psychosis – they will worsen parkinsonism and risk NMS. Use quetiapine or clozapine only after specialist input.
- **Do not dismiss new confusion/fall** as “just part of PD” – rule out infection (UTI, pneumonia), silent aspiration, or NMS.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Fever or rigors (possible aspiration or NMS)
  - Worsening confusion, drowsiness, or rigidity
  - Inability to swallow oral medications or keep food down
  - New falls or difficulty walking
- **Follow-up:** Parkinson’s clinic within 2–4 weeks; sooner if on newly started medication or after hospitalisation.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 or rising lactate
  - GCS drop ≤ 12 (suspect NMS, stroke, or sepsis)
  - Hyperthermia + rigidity + altered mental status (suspected NMS)
  - Cannot protect airway (aspiration, severe cough impairment)
